result:
I’ve been thinking about how the moon is a liar.

It hangs there night after night, pretending to be small enough to pinch between two fingers, small enough to tuck into a coat pocket, when really it’s a vast and airless desert on the brink of falling forever. We tilt our heads back and say “look, a dime in the sky,” and it lets us. It never brags about the oceans it drags around like obedient dogs, never mentions that it once saved Earth from a bullet storm of asteroids by stepping in front of them like a silent bodyguard. It just keeps faking tininess so we’ll keep looking up.

I envy that kind of modesty. Down here, everyone is shouting their actual size into the void, desperate to be measured correctly. We post calorie counts and salary histories and the exact number of steps we took between breakfast and grief. We want credit for surviving. Meanwhile the moon just swings outward and back, outward and back, gaining and losing the same few thousand kilometers like breath. It never once asks to be thanked for keeping the planet’s wobble in check, for making the nights bright enough that our ancestors didn’t lose every trail.

Yesterday I watched a man on the subway argue with his ex-girlfriend through a playlist. He queued ten songs in a row—each one a different shade of “you wrecked me”—and let them speak in order. By the time we reached 14th Street, the whole car understood the architecture of their ruin. She wasn’t even there; he was wearing earbuds. But the moonlight that leaked through the windows every time we crossed the river made it feel sanctioned, like a cathedral of public transport. Nobody told him to lower the volume. Sometimes witness is the only sacrament left.

I got home and tried to write something that didn’t mention the moon, just to see if I could. The page filled with tides instead. I wrote about a laundromat that only accepts sea glass as payment, about a bilingual stop sign that apologizes in both languages, about the way my father used to whistle entire symphonies while scraping ice off the windshield—little mercy killings of frost. Somewhere between the second and third stanza the moon slipped in disguised as a period, small and silver and impossible to delete. I gave up and let it stay.

Freud said the uncanny is the familiar made strange, but he never rode an elevator that sang. Mine croons a lullaby in 7/8 time; the security cam footage shows people swaying unconsciously, strangers becoming metronomes together. Westep, catch, breathe, step, catch, breathe. When the doors open, we scatter back into separate stories, but for thirty seconds we were one organism with eight hearts. The moon, meantime, keeps its same face turned toward us—an introvert at the party who pretends to check the time but is really taking group selfies with the mirror across the room.

I think that’s why I keep writing about it: the moon is the only ex I’m allowed to still love. It left me with tides, with werewolf metaphors, with the residual brightness that lets me find the bedroom door at 3 a.m. without stubbing my toe. It never texts back, but it never unfollows. It shows up thin or swollen, depending on how much of itself it’s willing to share, and I rearrange my insomnia accordingly. Some nights I open the window and speak aloud the names of everyone I’m scared I’ll forget. The moon keeps them in alphabetical craters; I have no proof, but it feels true.

Tonight it’s waxing gibbous—gibbous, such a word of hunger, of having more than half but never the whole meal. I stand on the fire escape and let it paint me counterfeit silver. Across the alley, a woman is teaching her parakeet to say “I matter.” The bird repeats, “I madder,” over and over, a syllable off, a confession. We’re all just trying to convince the dark to take us seriously. Up there, the moon rehearses disappearance, shaving itself nightly until it can try on the costume of nothing. Then, slowly, renaissance: a shoulder blade of light, a thumbnail, an eye. It knows the recipe for coming back so well it never bothers to write it down.

I tell myself: be more like that. Shed, return, don’t post about the process. But my phone buzzes with headlines—glaciers speed-running extinction, another city learning the dialect of flood. Down here, absence doesn’t always rewind. Sometimes it’s just absence, an orbit that decayed, a species that forgot how to be afraid of the wrong things. The moon keeps its trauma to itself; we call its scars “seas” as if they were once hospitable. Language, too, is a kind of orbital mechanics: we name the impact so it can’t name us.

So I write, because naming is the only gravity I own. I write about subway conductors who apologize for delays in the voice of a mother calming a nightmare, about the vending machine on 6th that dispenses tiny origami cranes for a dollar, about the way my dead dog still occupies forty-three percent of the bed, an invisible geography I refuse to roll over onto. Between sentences, I check the sky through the slit of a blind. Still there, still faking smallness. Good. Keep lying, I whisper. Keep us looking up so we remember the scale of wonder. Keep folding the tides like love letters we haven’t earned. When you finally drift too far away, may your empty throne in the sky be the shape of everything we forgot to thank you for. And may we, left finally alone with the dark, learn to generate our own pale light—something faint, steady, impossible to pocket, yet big enough to bleed through the curtains and guide a stranger home.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
